---
title: "Introduction, Exponential Smoothing & Forecast Evaluation"
subtitle: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting --- Lecture 1"
author: "Department of Economics"
institute: "University of Nebraska at Omaha"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    logo: ""
    footer: "BSAD 8310 | University of Nebraska at Omaha"
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date
---

## Lecture Outline

1. Why Forecasting Matters
2. Exponential Smoothing
3. Forecast Evaluation
4. Course Roadmap

---

# Why Forecasting Matters

Every business decision made today depends on expectations about tomorrow.

---

## Forecasting Is Everywhere in Business

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Operations & Supply Chain**

- How much inventory should Walmart order for Black Friday?
- How many nurses does a hospital need next Tuesday night?
- When will this machine component fail?

**Finance & Risk**

- Will credit card defaults rise next quarter?
- What will the Fed Funds rate be in 6 months?
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Marketing & Strategy**

- How many units will this new product sell in year one?
- What is the customer lifetime value of this cohort?

**Macro & Public Policy**

- Will GDP grow by 2% or 3% next year?

::: {.keybox}
A decision is only as good as the forecast it rests on.
:::
:::
::::

---

## The Basic Setup

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Time Series
:::
A **time series** is an ordered sequence of observations $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T\}$ where $t$ indexes time.
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**Key notation:**

- $y_t$ — observed value at time $t$
- $h$ — **forecast horizon** (periods ahead)
- $\hat{y}_{t+h|t}$ — forecast of $y_{t+h}$ made at time $t$
- $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1}$ — **forecast error**
:::
::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Example
:::
Forecasting monthly retail sales:

- $h=1$: next month's sales
- $h=12$: sales 12 months from now

Harder as $h$ grows — uncertainty compounds.
:::
:::
::::

::: {.keybox}
Under squared error loss, the optimal forecast is the **conditional expectation**: $\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \mathbb{E}[y_{t+h} \mid \mathcal{F}_t]$
:::

---

## Components of a Time Series

Additive decomposition: $y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
- **$T_t$: Trend** — long-run direction
- **$S_t$: Seasonality** — regular calendar pattern
- **$C_t$: Cycle** — medium-run business swings
- **$I_t$: Irregular** — random shocks
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Which Components Matter?
:::
- **Retail sales:** trend + seasonality
- **Quarterly GDP:** trend + cycle
- **Daily stock returns:** mostly irregular
- **Energy demand:** all four components
:::
:::
::::

::: {.warningbox}
**Ignoring seasonality causes large, systematic errors.** A non-seasonal model applied to monthly retail data will consistently under-forecast in December and over-forecast in January.
:::

---

## Always Beat a Benchmark First

::: {.keybox}
Before presenting any forecast model, check that it beats a **naive benchmark**. A model that cannot beat the baseline has zero value.
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Standard benchmarks:**

| Name | Formula |
|------|---------|
| Naïve | $\hat{y}_{t+h\|t} = y_t$ |
| Seasonal naïve | $\hat{y}_{t+h\|t} = y_{t+h-m}$ |
| Mean | $\hat{y}_{t+h\|t} = \bar{y}_t$ |
| Drift | $\hat{y}_{t+h\|t} = y_t + h\hat{c}$ |
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Which to Use?
:::
- No trend, no season → Mean
- Trend, no season → Drift
- Season → Seasonal naïve
- Asset prices → Naïve
:::

[The M4 Competition: many ML models *failed* to beat simple ETS methods [@Makridakis2020].]{.neutral .smaller}
:::
::::

---

# Exponential Smoothing

Recent observations should carry more weight than old ones.

---

## The Problem with Equal Weights

Most classical methods treat every historical observation equally — but what if the world **changes**?

::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Business Context
:::
A retailer's sales pattern shifts after a store renovation. A method that treats pre-renovation data equally will take months to adapt, systematically over- or under-forecasting throughout.
:::

**Exponential weighting idea:** assign geometrically declining weights to past observations.

| Observation | Age | Weight |
|------------|-----|--------|
| $y_T$ | 0 | $\alpha$ |
| $y_{T-1}$ | 1 | $\alpha(1-\alpha)$ |
| $y_{T-2}$ | 2 | $\alpha(1-\alpha)^2$ |

[Parameter $\alpha \in (0,1]$ controls how fast old data is **forgotten**. High $\alpha$ = short memory; low $\alpha$ = long memory.]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
SES Update Equation
:::
Level at time $t$: $\ell_t = \alpha\, y_t + (1-\alpha)\,\ell_{t-1}$, $\quad 0 < \alpha \leq 1$

**Forecast:** $\hat{y}_{T+h|T} = \ell_T$ for all horizons $h \geq 1$ (flat forecast)
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Large $\alpha$ (e.g. 0.8):**

- Reacts quickly to shocks
- Noisy, volatile forecasts
- Good for rapidly changing series
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Small $\alpha$ (e.g. 0.1):**

- Slow to adapt
- Smooth, stable forecasts
- Good for slowly evolving series
:::
::::

::: {.keybox}
Special cases: $\alpha = 1$ ⟹ naïve forecast. $\alpha \to 0$ ⟹ historical mean. SES **nests** both benchmarks.
:::

[Use when: no trend, no seasonality.]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Holt's Method: Adding Trend

SES produces a *flat* forecast. If the series has a trend, track both level and slope.

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Holt's Linear Exponential Smoothing
:::
**Level:** $\ell_t = \alpha\, y_t + (1-\alpha)\,(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$

**Trend:** $b_t = \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)\,b_{t-1}$

**Forecast:** $\hat{y}_{T+h|T} = \ell_T + h\,b_T$
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.warningbox}
**Linear extrapolation can be unrealistic for long horizons.** Trends rarely continue forever. Use the **damped trend** for $h > 6$:

$$\hat{y}_{T+h|T} = \ell_T + \sum_{j=1}^{h}\phi^j b_T$$

where $0 < \phi < 1$ causes the trend to taper off.
:::
:::
::: {.column width="46%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Interpretation
:::
- $\ell_t$: where the series *is* now
- $b_t$: the current growth rate
- $\phi = 0.85$: long-run forecast converges to a finite ceiling rather than growing forever
:::
:::
::::

---

## Holt-Winters: Level, Trend, and Seasonality

For series with *both* trend and seasonality, add a third component.

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Holt-Winters Additive Method
:::
$\ell_t = \alpha(y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1}+b_{t-1})$

$b_t = \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)\,b_{t-1}$

$s_t = \gamma(y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma)\,s_{t-m}$

**Forecast:** $\hat{y}_{T+h|T} = \ell_T + h\,b_T + s_{T+h-m}$ &emsp; ($m$ = seasonal period)
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Additive vs. Multiplicative:**

- **Additive:** seasonal swings are constant in size
- **Multiplicative:** seasonal swings grow with the level (use for retail, tourism)
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
US Retail Sales
:::
The December holiday spike grows each year as overall sales grow ⟹ **multiplicative seasonality**.
:::
[Requires ≥ 2m observations to initialize; $m=12$ for monthly data.]{.neutral .smaller}
:::
::::

---

## The ETS Framework: A Unified View

::: {.interpretation}
*Hyndman et al. (2021) unify all exponential smoothing models as **state space models**: Error × Trend × Seasonal.*
:::

| Component | Options | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Error ($E$) | A, M | Additive / Multiplicative |
| Trend ($T$) | N, A, A~d~ | None / Additive / Damped |
| Seasonal ($S$) | N, A, M | None / Additive / Multiplicative |

15 valid combinations. Key examples:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **ETS(A,N,N)** = SES
- **ETS(A,A,N)** = Holt linear
- **ETS(A,A~d~,N)** = Holt damped
:::
::: {.column width="46%"}
- **ETS(A,A,A)** = Holt-Winters additive
- **ETS(A,A,M)** = Holt-Winters multiplicative
:::
::::

::: {.keybox}
**Automatic selection:** fit all 15 models; pick minimum AIC.
In Python: `statsmodels.tsa.exponential_smoothing.ets.ETSModel`
:::

---

# Forecast Evaluation

Forecasting without evaluation is not science — it is storytelling.

---

## How Do We Measure Forecast Accuracy?

Given out-of-sample errors $e_{T+h} = y_{T+h} - \hat{y}_{T+h|T}$:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Scale-dependent (same series):**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{H}\sum_{h=1}^{H} e_{T+h}^2}$$

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{H}\sum_{h=1}^{H} |e_{T+h}|$$

[RMSE penalizes large errors more heavily. If big misses are especially costly (e.g., stockouts), prefer RMSE.]{.neutral .smaller}
:::
::: {.column width="46%"}
**Scale-free (cross-series):**

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{H}\sum_{h=1}^{H} \left|\frac{e_{T+h}}{y_{T+h}}\right|$$

$$\text{MASE} = \frac{\text{MAE}}{\text{MAE}_{\text{seasonal naïve}}}$$

::: {.keybox}
MASE < 1: beats seasonal naïve.

MASE > 1: [worse]{.negative} than naïve.

Standard in M-Competitions.
:::
:::
::::

---

## The Golden Rule: Always Evaluate Out-of-Sample

::: {.keybox}
**Never evaluate a forecast model on the data used to fit it.** In-sample fit measures how well the model memorized the past, not how well it can predict the future.
:::

**Correct time series train/test split:**

```
|←————— Training set (t = 1 to T) ————→|←— Test set →|
                                         ↑
                                   Forecast origin
```

**Correct Python approach:**
```python
from sklearn.model_selection import TimeSeriesSplit
tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)
```

::: {.warningbox}
**Never use `KFold` on time series data.** Shuffling allows future data to "train" the model — a form of data leakage that makes results artificially good. Always use `TimeSeriesSplit`.
:::

---

# Course Roadmap

---

## BSAD 8310: Four-Part Structure

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Part I: Time Series Models**

1. Intro, ETS & Evaluation ← *today*
2. ARIMA & VAR
3. Generalized Additive Models

**Part II: Tree-Based Models**

4. Decision Trees
5. Random Forests
6. Boosted Trees & Regularization
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Part III: Deep Learning**

7. Neural Networks (PyTorch)
8. CNN Architectures
9. RNNs, LSTMs & Transformers

**Part IV: Bayesian Models**

10. Bayesian Statistics I: Foundations
11. Bayesian Statistics II: Time Series
12. Bayesian Statistics III: Linear Regression
:::
::::

[Python throughout: statsmodels, scikit-learn, xgboost, PyTorch, prophet, pyGAM, pymc.]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Tools and What You Will Be Able to Do

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Python libraries:**

- `statsmodels` — ETS, ARIMA, VAR
- `scikit-learn` — ML models, pipelines, CV
- `xgboost` — gradient boosting
- `torch` (PyTorch) — neural networks
- `prophet` / `pygam` — GAMs
- `pymc` — Bayesian models
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**By the end of this course:**

- [✓]{.positive} Choose the right method for a business problem
- [✓]{.positive} Implement and tune models in Python
- [✓]{.positive} Evaluate models rigorously out-of-sample
- [✓]{.positive} Interpret results for non-technical stakeholders
- [✓]{.positive} Use AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude) to accelerate analysis
- [✓]{.positive} Avoid the most common forecasting mistakes
:::
::::

---

## Lecture 1: Key Takeaways

::: {.keybox}
1. Forecasting underpins every forward-looking business decision. The optimal point forecast (under squared loss) is the **conditional expectation**.

2. **Always beat a benchmark first.** Naïve, seasonal naïve, mean, drift.

3. Exponential smoothing assigns **geometrically declining weights** to past observations. $\alpha$ controls how fast old data is forgotten.

4. SES (level) → Holt (+ trend) → Holt-Winters (+ season). The **ETS framework** unifies all variants; AIC selects automatically.

5. **Evaluate out-of-sample only.** RMSE penalizes large errors; MASE benchmarks against seasonal naïve. Never shuffle time series data.
:::

*Next: ARIMA & VAR — a regression-based approach to time series with richer autocorrelation structure.*

---

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
